questions.question: A line that cuts a figure into two parts that exactly overlap when folded along that line is called:
questions.themeWrapper: ⚔️ QUEST CHALLENGE — The Gate of Reflection stands before you! Answer correctly to pass:

questions.question: A square piece of paper is folded vertically, horizontally, and along both diagonals. How many lines of symmetry does a square have in total?
questions.themeWrapper: 🛡️ QUEST TRIAL — The Ancient Square Shield reveals its secrets. How many axes of power does it possess?

questions.question: A paper windmill is rotated about its centre. At which angle does it first look exactly the same as its original position (other than 360°)?
questions.themeWrapper: 🌀 QUEST SPIN — The Enchanted Windmill of Rotational Power is before you! At what angle does it first reveal its hidden symmetry?

questions.question: What is the difference between line symmetry and rotational symmetry? Give one example of a shape that has rotational symmetry but NO line of symmetry.
questions.themeWrapper: 📜 QUEST SCROLL — The Twin Tomes of Symmetry lie before the young hero. Decipher the difference between the two ancient arts and name a shape that wields only one of them!
questions.sampleAnswer: Line symmetry (reflection symmetry) means a figure can be folded along a line so that both halves overlap exactly. Rotational symmetry means a figure looks exactly the same after being rotated by a certain angle (less than 360°) about a fixed centre point. A paper windmill (pinwheel) is an example of a shape that has rotational symmetry (angles of symmetry: 90°, 180°, 270°, 360°) but has no line of symmetry, because folding it does not produce two overlapping halves.

questions.question: When an ink blot is created by folding a paper, spilling ink on one half, and pressing the halves together, the resulting figure is symmetric. Where is the line of symmetry in such a figure, and why does this method always produce a symmetric figure?
questions.themeWrapper: 🔮 QUEST RIDDLE — The Ink Blot Oracle speaks! Brave adventurer, explain why the Oracle's method always conjures a perfectly symmetric pattern and where its line of power lies.
questions.sampleAnswer: The line of symmetry in the ink blot figure is the fold line of the paper. This method always produces a symmetric figure because the ink on one half is pressed onto the other half, creating an exact mirror image. The fold acts as the line of reflection — whatever shape appears on one side is perfectly mirrored on the other side, making both halves identical. Therefore, the fold line is always the line of symmetry.

questions.question: A regular hexagon (a hexagon with all equal sides and equal angles) is given to you. (a) How many lines of symmetry does a regular hexagon have? Describe where each line of symmetry is located. (b) What are all the angles of rotational symmetry for a regular hexagon? (c) Is the diagonal of a rectangle (that is NOT a square) a line of symmetry? Explain why or why not using the concept of mirror halves.
questions.themeWrapper: 🏆 FINAL QUEST — The Grand Symmetry Citadel has three sealed doors. Only the mightiest mathematical hero who can answer all three parts will unlock the treasure within. Draw upon your knowledge and conquer!
questions.sampleAnswer: (a) A regular hexagon has 6 lines of symmetry. Three lines pass through opposite vertices (corner to corner), and three lines pass through the midpoints of opposite sides (mid-side to mid-side). Each line divides the hexagon into two mirror halves that overlap exactly when folded. (b) The angles of rotational symmetry for a regular hexagon are: 60°, 120°, 180°, 240°, 300°, and 360°. This is because the hexagon has 6 equal parts, and 360° ÷ 6 = 60°, so every multiple of 60° is an angle of symmetry — giving 6 angles in total. (c) The diagonal of a rectangle that is NOT a square is NOT a line of symmetry. When we fold the rectangle along its diagonal, the two parts do not overlap each other completely — one triangular part extends beyond the other. This is because the length and width of a non-square rectangle are different, so the two triangular halves created by the diagonal are not mirror images of each other. Only if the rectangle were a square (where length equals width) would the diagonal serve as a line of symmetry.
